name: Bennu
description: The Bennu is an ancient Egyptian deity linked with the sun, creation, and rebirth. Often depicted as a heron or eagle with golden or red plumage, it was believed to be the soul of Ra, the sun god, or an aspect of Osiris. The Bennu's origins are tied to the city of Heliopolis, a major center of sun worship. According to myths, the Bennu was self-created from a fire that burned at the top of the Benben stone, a pyramidion-shaped stone associated with the creation mound. It was believed to have the power to regenerate itself, rising from the ashes much like the phoenix of Greek mythology, which is likely derived from the Bennu. The Bennu's cry was thought to have marked the beginning of time, and it was associated with the inundation of the Nile, bringing fertility and prosperity to the land. Temples dedicated to the sun god often featured depictions of the Bennu, emphasizing its connection to the cycle of life, death, and rebirth. The Bennu represents hope and renewal, a symbol of overcoming adversity and the promise of a brighter future. Its link to Ra solidifies its association with light, warmth, and the life-giving power of the sun. Furthermore, its connection to Osiris links it to the afterlife and the promise of resurrection. The Bennu's role in Egyptian cosmology is significant, embodying the enduring power of creation and the cyclical nature of existence. It is a powerful reminder of the Egyptians' belief in the eternal cycle of life and the potential for rebirth after death.
subtitle: The Egyptian Phoenix of Rebirth

keyMyths.title: Self-Creation
keyMyths.description: The Bennu was believed to have created itself from a fire atop the Benben stone in Heliopolis. This act symbolized the beginning of time and the power of self-renewal.

keyMyths.title: Association with Ra
keyMyths.description: The Bennu was often seen as the soul of Ra, the sun god, representing his daily cycle of death and rebirth. Its appearance heralded the dawn and the sun's return.

keyMyths.title: Connection to Osiris
keyMyths.description: The Bennu was also associated with Osiris, the god of the afterlife, symbolizing resurrection and eternal life. This linked it to the promise of rebirth after death.

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
sources.work: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions of Ancient Egypt
